title: SEGMENT 8 — Mogamulizumab — the first afucosylated drug approved

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Mogamulizumab — the first afucosylated drug approved
Mogamulizumab.
Hard name to say.
Let's break it down: moga-mulizu-mab.
The "-mab" suffix tells you it's a monoclonal antibody.
The "-mu-" tells you it's humanized.
And the "mogamuli" is just a coined stem.
The trade name is Poteligeo, and the drug was developed by Kyowa Hakko Kirin — the same company that built Potelligent — using their own platform on their own target.
This was the proof-of-concept drug for the entire afucose class.
The target is CCR4 — chemokine receptor 4.
CCR4 is a G-protein-coupled receptor expressed on certain types of T cells.
Specifically, CCR4 is expressed on regulatory T cells and on a subset of effector T cells called Th2 cells.

And critically for our story, CCR4 is also highly expressed on the malignant T cells in two related diseases: adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL) and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL), including the subtypes mycosis fungoides and Sézary syndrome.
These are nasty cancers.
ATLL is a complication of infection with the human T-cell leukemia virus (HTLV-1), endemic in parts of Japan, the Caribbean, and Africa.
CTCL is a chronic, often disfiguring skin lymphoma that presents with itchy, scaly patches and plaques on the skin and can progress to systemic disease.
Both diseases are characterized by malignant T-cell populations that express CCR4 on their surface, and both diseases have, historically, been poorly served by available therapies.
Median survival in ATLL was measured in months.
CTCL patients went through cycle after cycle of phototherapy, skin-directed treatments, oral retinoids, single-agent chemotherapies, and often progressed regardless.
The clinical rationale for an anti-CCR4 antibody is straightforward.
The malignant T cells display CCR4 abundantly.
An antibody that binds CCR4 and recruits NK-mediated killing should preferentially eliminate the malignant cells.

The catch is that CCR4 is also expressed on regulatory T cells and Th2 cells, so an aggressive anti-CCR4 antibody will also deplete those populations — and that has consequences for immune tolerance and skin barrier function that we'll get to.
Now, here's the question: why did Kyowa Hakko make mogamulizumab afucosylated?
The answer is that, compared to a fucosylated anti-CCR4 antibody, the afucose version killed CCR4-bearing T cells dramatically harder in preclinical assays.

The team chose CCR4 partly because it was an unmet medical need in a CHO-engineered platform looking for a target, and partly because the biology lent itself perfectly to ADCC enhancement: the malignant cells were sensitive to NK killing, the target was abundant, and the boost from afucose would be expected to translate directly into clinical efficacy.
The clinical trials in Japan, starting in the late 2000s, were striking.
In relapsed/refractory ATLL — a disease where existing salvage therapies gave response rates in the single digits — mogamulizumab gave response rates of 50% in some studies.
Some patients achieved durable remissions.
The drug was approved in Japan in 2012 for relapsed/refractory ATLL, then expanded to CCR4-positive peripheral T-cell lymphoma and CTCL.
In the United States, mogamulizumab was approved in 2018 for adults with relapsed or refractory mycosis fungoides or Sézary syndrome — the two principal forms of CTCL.
The pivotal trial, called MAVORIC, compared mogamulizumab head-to-head against vorinostat, an HDAC inhibitor that was the standard salvage therapy.
Mogamulizumab won, with significantly longer progression-free survival, particularly in Sézary patients.
The result was clear enough that the FDA approved on this evidence.
Mogamulizumab matters in our story for two reasons.

First, it was the first FDA-approved afucosylated antibody — the proof-of-concept that a glycoengineered drug could go through full regulatory development, demonstrate clinical benefit, and reach patients.

Second, it validated the central premise of the Potelligent platform: that an antibody made in FUT8-knockout CHO would have manufacturing characteristics indistinguishable from a conventional antibody, but clinical potency dramatically higher than the fucosylated version would have shown.
There are limitations and side effects worth noting.

Mogamulizumab depletes regulatory T cells along with the malignant T cells — because both populations express CCR4 — and that depletion can lead to immune-related skin reactions, sometimes severe enough that patients can't continue treatment.
There's a particular concern in allogeneic stem-cell transplant settings, where depletion of host or donor Tregs can predispose to graft-versus-host disease.
So mogamulizumab is potent, and the afucose engineering is part of what makes it potent, but the same potency carries risks.
This is a theme we'll see again with obinutuzumab.
Mogamulizumab opened the door.
The drug that walked through next, and arguably became the more commercially important glycoengineered antibody, was obinutuzumab.
